title: Children of Light
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Children of Light - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 5_6-14.txt

outline.title: Connecting Ephesians 5:6-14 to the Preceding Passage
subpoints: Walking in love (Eph 5:1-5) primarily governs conduct toward other Christians
Walking as children of light (Eph 5:6-14) primarily governs conduct toward unbelievers
The exhortation to expose works of darkness situates Paul's concern outward
The two walks complement each other: love within, light without

outline.title: The Danger of Being Deceived by Empty Words
subpoints: Satan's one strategy is to cause God's people to doubt God's Word: 'Has God really said?'
Empty words come not from a talking serpent but from friends, family, media, and even well-meaning leaders
The wrath of God falls on the sons of disobedience — sin is serious and not to be minimized
Christians must not be partakers with the sons of disobedience regardless of cultural pressure

outline.title: Identity as the Foundation of Obedience
subpoints: Paul's recurring strategy throughout Ephesians: remind believers of who they are in Christ
The Christian is not merely 'in' the light — he or she is light itself (Eph 5:8)
Apart from Christ we were darkness itself, not merely trapped in it — a total condition
Knowing our new identity in Christ gives boldness to resist temptation

outline.title: The Fruit of the Light: Goodness, Righteousness, and Truth
subpoints: The singular fruit of the light comprises three dimensions: goodness, righteousness, and truth
Goodness denotes moral excellence combined with a generous spirit, growing over a lifetime
Righteousness means doing what is right; truth means honesty and genuineness
Finding out what is acceptable to the Lord comes through walking closer to God and His Word

outline.title: How Christians Shine as Light in the World
subpoints: We are lighthouses: the light is in us, not from us; the source is Christ, the Light of the world
A city on a hill cannot be hidden — the gospel light shines even in deep cultural darkness
Covering the light is the greatest waste imaginable; true followers burst at the seams to shine
Light brings glory to God both through conversions and through the persecution it provokes

outline.title: Ordinary Christian Life as the Means of Shining
subpoints: Shining is not achieved by extraordinary feats but by the 10,000 ordinary moments of faithful Christian living
Faithful marriages, honest speech, peacemaking, love of neighbor, sincere worship — all shine
Obedience to parents, integrity at work, and humble prayer are each individual rays of focused light
The accumulated ordinary obedience of the church creates a magnificent light the world cannot ignore

summary:
In this sermon from Ephesians 5:6-14, Pastor Tito Lyro continues the congregation's walk through the book of Ephesians, focusing on Paul's exhortation to walk as children of light. He begins by connecting this passage to the preceding section: while Ephesians 5:1-5 governs how Christians are to walk in love toward one another within the church, verses 6-14 are primarily addressed outward, governing how believers relate to the unbelieving world around them.

The heart of the sermon centers on the danger of being deceived by empty words. Paul anticipated that someone would minimize the sins he listed in verses 3-5, and Pastor Tito identifies this as Satan's one unchanging strategy since Genesis 3: 'Has God really said?' These empty words do not come from a talking serpent but from friends, family members, songs, celebrities, and social media. The antidote is a firm grasp of Christian identity. Paul does not say that believers are 'in' the light — he says they are light. Just as unregenerate humanity is not merely trapped in darkness but is itself darkness, the regenerate believer has been made light in Christ. This identity transforms temptation: you do not have to follow through on sin because that is no longer who you are.

The sermon then traces the fruit of the light — goodness, righteousness, and truth — as the evidence that believers are walking consistently with their identity. Drawing on Matthew 5:14-16, Pastor Tito explains that Christians are lighthouses, not light sources: the light is in them because Christ, the Light of the world, dwells in them. The light shines not through spectacular achievements but through the accumulated 10,000 ordinary moments of faithful Christian living: obedient children, honest workers, faithful marriages, peacemakers, and humble worshipers. When these rays are focused together, the world sees a brilliant light it cannot ignore, bringing glory to God whether through conversions or through the persecution the light inevitably provokes.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 5:6-14

If you're able, you remain standing a moment longer and we're going to turn to Ephesians chapter 5. We're going to start being in verse 6. And as you do that, I'll tell you next Lord's Day, Lord willing, Elder Anderson's going to teach us regarding Jay Gresham Meichen who is a the good guy in the one of the good guys in the great controversy that gave birth to our church. We'll be our Sunday school lesson on YouTube and this, him we just sang is one of his favorite hymns but more importantly to us is also one of Leo Zasas's favorite a hymn which is more important than being Meichen's favorite hymn at least as far as we are concerned.

*Let no one deceive you with empty words for because of these things the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. Therefore do not be partakers with them. For you are once darkness but now you are light in the Lord. Walk as children of light for the fruit of the Spirit is in all goodness, righteousness and truth finding out what is acceptable to the Lord and have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness but rather expose them. For it is shameful even to speak of those things which are done by them in secret but all things that are exposed are made manifest by the light for whatever makes manifest is light. Therefore he says, awake you who sleep, arise from the dead and Christ will give you light.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together.

*Lord, we pray, give us light as we consider this portion of your word for us in Jesus name. Amen.*

Please be seated.

## Connecting to the Previous Passage

As I told you last week I was struggling with a formality in this passage into a sermon and the result of that was actually two sermons out of this passage. So the Lord willing next week will return to this passage. We are currently navigating through the walking portion of the book of Ephesians. We could go all the way back to chapter two saying that that's the walking passage of the book of Ephesians. But here in chapter five, three times the apostle tells us that we are to walk in a certain way. In verse two he tells us that we are to walk in love. In verse eight we are to walk as children of light and in verse 15 we are to walk wisely. And as I said many times before this idea of walking is really the idea of living, the idea of behaving. We are to live in a certain way because of who we are in Christ.

And with this idea of walk once again we see that the Christian life is an active life. It's not a passive life, it's not something that you just do sitting down. You actively pursue Christ. The Christian life is a life of movement and growth as opposed to being a static or stagnant life.

And this morning I would like to do three things as we consider this passage. First I would like for us to briefly connect verses 6 through 14 to what's gone before in verses 1 through 5 of chapter five. Then I would like us to see that a Christian is not to participate in the sins of unbelievers because he or she is not an unbeliever. And then lastly I would like us to understand how we can positively be light in a dead world.

So let's first take a look at how what we read today verses 6 through 14 connects to what we considered two weeks ago in verses 1 through 5. And what we see here is that Paul says that walking in love mainly describes the manner of conduct of Christians toward other Christians. In verses 1 through 5 Paul describes what it means to walk in love. And when he does that he is saying this is how Christians should behave toward other Christians.

Now this is not to say that we are not to love the unbeliever. The command to love our neighbor as ourselves alone is enough to dismiss any attitude that tells us that we don't owe any love to the people outside of the church. All I'm saying is that the focus of verses 1 through 5 is in relationship to one another.

Now when we come to verse 8 and to the exhortation to walk as children of light, Paul is now emphasizing our conduct toward those who are outside of the church. Walking in love toward those who are in the church, walk as children of light to those who are outside of the church. And I conclude that because of the reference to exposing the unfruitful works of darkness in verse 11 and again the exposing effect of the light in verse 13. So the section that we are considering this morning primarily guides us through our relationship with the unbeliever in world.

## The Danger of Deception by Empty Words

And what Paul says is that we are not to participate in the sins of unbelievers because we are not unbelievers. Paul is willing to state the obvious. He says don't sin because you are not part of the unbeliever world. Don't behave as if you are not a Christian because you are a Christian. That should be so obvious to us but we often fail to follow that.

And what we see here is that Paul being an expert on human nature anticipated that some Christians would give heed to the idea that the sins that he enumerated in verses three through five weren't that bad — that it would be okay for Christians to be involved in them. Look at verses six and seven. Let no one deceive you with empty words for because of these things the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. Therefore do not be partakers with them.

Paul being an expert in human nature knew that somebody was going to say, well it's not quite like Paul is saying. You don't have to be that serious. It's okay to be sexually immoral. It's okay to be covetous. But Paul was also an expert on how Satan behaves. He knew that Satan had one and only one strategy. And that strategy is to cause God's people to doubt God's word. That's how he's operated from the very beginning.

He knew that Satan was going to avail himself of this strategy to come to the Ephesians or to us today and say, has God really said — is that really as bad as God is saying? And Paul anticipated that because that's how Satan behaves.

Remember the story, the account of Satan tempting Eve in Genesis chapter 3, verses 1 to 6. The serpent was more cunning than any beast of the field which the Lord God had made. And he said to the woman, has God indeed said, you shall not eat of every tree of the garden? See the subtlety there? He didn't come to dismiss what God said outright. He just planted that seed. Has God really said? Is that really what God meant?

And when Satan works in our lives today, he's not going to come as a talking serpent. These empty words that Paul is referring to here — the words that deceive — don't come from the mouth of a talking serpent. These words are going to come from the mouth of a friend. They're going to come from a boyfriend or a girlfriend. They're going to come from a family member. They're coming from the lyrics of a song that we like. They might even come from well-meaning Christian leaders. Satan has not changed his strategy and is going to try to deceive you and me into thinking that what God says in his word is not the best for us.

But our God is so good, our God is so merciful, that he mercifully warns us that he is very serious about what he says, as demonstrated by the consequence in verse 6: the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. The wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. What is the consequence of routinely not walking as light? The consequence is the wrath of God and that's serious. It took Christ the Son of God in the flesh to cause the wrath of God to be removed from upon you and me. Don't disregard that. Sin is serious.

## Identity as the Foundation: You Are Light

As Paul continues, he returns to something that he's been returning to all the time through the epistle. He returns to the matter of our identity. The way to be faithful to God, the way to be strengthened in obedience to God is to remember who we are in Jesus Christ. Look at verse 8. For you were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord — walk as children of light.

He has been doing this all the way from chapter two. Always reminding us — don't do this, don't do that, and why? Because of who you are in Jesus Christ.

And I want you to notice something very important. He doesn't say you were once in darkness. He says you were once darkness itself. The one who is unregenerate is not simply trapped in darkness as a hostage wanting to be redeemed. He or she is part of the fabric of darkness itself.

But we are no longer that anymore. The picture that came to mind as I was working on this sermon is of a Dementor from Harry Potter — that soul-sucking, joy-sucking, life-destroying being. When the Dementor enters the room, everybody knows it. The life is sucked out of everything. That's a great picture of the one without Christ: not as an innocent hostage, but as darkness himself, darkness herself, sucking the life of everything around them.

That's who you were. That's who I was apart from Christ. But that's not who we are anymore.

## The Fruit of the Light

Notice also that Paul doesn't describe the Christian as being in the light, but as being light itself. Can you imagine that? God says to the believer: you are light. And the only way for the world to know light is through you.

Because we are children of light, we live and walk according to who we are, according to our identity. And the measure of whether we are walking in the light is the fruit that our life produces. Look at verse 9. For the fruit of the light is in all goodness, righteousness and truth.

This threefold fruit — it is a single, singular word, the word for fruit. It's not that sometimes we have goodness, sometimes righteousness, sometimes truth. This is one fruit described by three words as evidence that we are children of light.

Goodness means achievement of moral excellence combined with a generous spirit — and this does not mean perfection. It does mean that this is the direction of our lives. As we look at the movie of our lives, we are growing in goodness. We are also growing in righteousness — it just means doing what is right. And truth here means honesty and genuineness. That is also growing in our lives.

The more we walk in the light, the more we grow in our walk with the Lord. Look at verse 10: finding out what is acceptable to the Lord. As we walk, as we live, we more and more know what the light of God is. In my study we have a framed verse in Psalm 36 verse 9: for with you is the fountain of life, in your light we see light. The closer we walk with God, the more light we see.

## How We Shine: The Light of Christ in Ordinary Life

Now what does it look like to positively walk as children of light? In essence, how do we shine? Turn to Matthew chapter 5. Look at verses 14 through 16. You are the light of the world. A city that is set on a hill cannot be hidden. Nor do they light a lamp and put it under a basket, but on a lamp stand, and it gives light to all who are in the house. Let your light so shine before men that they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven.

First, it is important that we understand that our light does not find its origin in us. Jesus is not saying you are so bright and good, let people see that. Some have used the illustration of a mirror reflecting the light of God — some truth to that. But I think a better analogy is that we are lighthouses. The light is in us and we are the lighthouses. The light is not us. The light is in us: the Lord Jesus Christ. God has shone that light in us.

And I want you to notice — Christ was emphasizing something. He is almost saying: you, and you, and you are the light. Not something else, not some other organization, but you — the Church of Jesus Christ is the light. And the word 'the' specifies there is this one light. There is this one light that the world will see, and that light is you, is me.

A city on the hill cannot be hidden. If you have traveled a very dark road at night with no street lighting and then you see a city in the distance — it glows on the sky and you can't hide it. Growing up my parents had a beach house on a fishing village about 50 miles outside of town. There was no street lighting. But when we looked toward the nearest city, the sky was all illuminated. There was no doubt — if we started walking, in a couple of days we'd walk into that city. That is the picture here.

Jeremiah 20 gives us a vivid picture of what it means to burst at the seams with the light of God. Jeremiah says: no Lord, every time I preach I get in trouble. I've been arrested, beaten, put in the mud. Preaching your word just gives me trouble. I'm going to stop. And then he says — but his word was in my heart like a burning fire shut up in my bones. I was weary of holding it back and I could not.

That is us. We are that city on the hill. And Jesus tells us that this light has a purpose in verse 16 — to bring glory to God. The world sees your light and glorifies God. Now that doesn't always mean they convert. Your light may cause the world to glorify God by destroying you, by making fun of you, by persecuting you. When we proclaim the gospel, to some it is the smell of life, but to others it is the stink of death.

How do we shine? Christ helps us see the answer throughout the Sermon on the Mount. A healthy regard for the doing and teaching of the whole counsel of God. A life of peacemaking and respect for those around you. Faithful marriages. Honest speech. Selflessness. Love for your neighbor. Sincere heart of worship. These are all ways that we shine our light.

And do you notice what they are? They are ordinary ways a Christian lives. These are not extraordinary feats. Christ says: walking in the light means just being what God calls you to be — living the ordinary everyday life of a Christian. It is in the 10,000 moments of little moments of life that the light of Christ shines most gloriously, because each one of those little moments is one little ray of light. And when you bring those 10,000 moments together, you have a focused bright light that everyone can see.

The Lord has brought us into his kingdom by his creative power. He infused us with his Spirit. And he chose to bring the nations to himself by having you and me be light — a demonstration of his character to the world. He says in 2 Corinthians 4:6-7 that he shone the light of God in our hearts, that we might hold it in jars of clay. Now why does Paul use that description? What are pitchers made for? They are made to pour what's inside into other vessels, other people. And that's what we're called to do — the ordinary day-to-day life of a follower of Christ. And that's going to create a massive, magnificent, focused light that the world will know belongs to a follower of Jesus.

*Father, thank you that you are God who gives us light. We pray that as we walk in your light, you give us even more light. For asking in Jesus name. Amen.*
